exercises.title: WebSocket subscriptions vs HTTP polling — comparison
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha has 5000 users watching NIFTY50 live prices. Compare:

Option A: HTTP Polling (every 500ms per user)
Option B: GraphQL Subscriptions (WebSocket, server pushes every 500ms)

1. How many HTTP requests/s in option A?
2. How many WebSocket connections in option B?
3. How many server pushes/s in option B?
4. At what user count does the difference become critical?
5. Which approach handles a network hiccup better?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. HTTP requests/second (Option A):
   5000 users × 2 requests/s (every 500ms) = 10,000 HTTP req/s
   Each request: TLS handshake + headers + auth + response
   Server: 10,000 connections opened and closed per second
   CPU/memory overhead: significant (each connection = thread + session)

2. WebSocket connections (Option B):
   5000 users → 5000 persistent WebSocket connections
   Each connection kept open: minimal overhead after handshake
   No repeated TLS handshakes (connection persists)

3. Server pushes/second (Option B):
   Price updates: 2 per second × 5000 users = 10,000 pushes/s
   BUT: with Flux.filter(), only push when price CHANGES
   NIFTY50: changes ~30 times per minute = 0.5 changes/s
   So: 5000 users × 0.5 changes/s = 2500 pushes/s (vs 10,000 requests)
   4× less traffic than polling, with lower latency!

4. Critical user count:
   HTTP polling: breaks at ~1000 users (10,000 req/s → server saturation)
   WebSocket: handles 10,000+ connections (persistent, low overhead per connection)
   Critical threshold: 500-1000 users where polling becomes a problem

5. Network hiccup handling:
   HTTP polling: hiccup → client retries on next poll interval (graceful, client-driven)
   WebSocket: hiccup → connection drops → client must reconnect + re-subscribe
   Advantage: HTTP polling is simpler (auto-recovery)
   WebSocket: needs reconnection logic (exponential backoff)
   Production: WebSocket clients implement auto-reconnect (Kite app does this)

test_cases.expected:
1. HTTP requests/second (Option A):
   5000 users × 2 requests/s (every 500ms) = 10,000 HTTP req/s
   Each request: TLS handshake + headers + auth + response
   Server: 10,000 connections opened and closed per second
   CPU/memory overhead: significant (each connection = thread + session)

2. WebSocket connections (Option B):
   5000 users → 5000 persistent WebSocket connections
   Each connection kept open: minimal overhead after handshake
   No repeated TLS handshakes (connection persists)

3. Server pushes/second (Option B):
   Price updates: 2 per second × 5000 users = 10,000 pushes/s
   BUT: with Flux.filter(), only push when price CHANGES
   NIFTY50: changes ~30 times per minute = 0.5 changes/s
   So: 5000 users × 0.5 changes/s = 2500 pushes/s (vs 10,000 requests)
   4× less traffic than polling, with lower latency!

4. Critical user count:
   HTTP polling: breaks at ~1000 users (10,000 req/s → server saturation)
   WebSocket: handles 10,000+ connections (persistent, low overhead per connection)
   Critical threshold: 500-1000 users where polling becomes a problem

5. Network hiccup handling:
   HTTP polling: hiccup → client retries on next poll interval (graceful, client-driven)
   WebSocket: hiccup → connection drops → client must reconnect + re-subscribe
   Advantage: HTTP polling is simpler (auto-recovery)
   WebSocket: needs reconnection logic (exponential backoff)
   Production: WebSocket clients implement auto-reconnect (Kite app does this)

hints: 10,000 HTTP req/s: significant server load
WebSocket: persistent connection, lower overhead
filter for changes: only push when price actually changes

exercises.title: Complete the @SubscriptionMapping resolver
exercises.instructions: Fill in the subscription resolver for Zerodha live prices.
hints: @SubscriptionMapping: subscription resolver annotation
Flux.interval(Duration.ofSeconds(1)): emit every 1 second
onBackpressureDrop: drop messages if subscriber is slow

exercises.title: Basic Flux-based subscription
exercises.instructions:
Write a simple price subscription:
1. PriceUpdate record: symbol, price, timestamp
2. @SubscriptionMapping priceUpdate(symbol): Flux.interval(1s) → PriceUpdate
3. .take(5): only 5 updates (finite for testing)
4. @SpringBootTest: Flux emits 5 elements correctly
hints: StepVerifier: Reactor's testing tool for Flux/Mono
.take(5): finite stream for testing (production subscriptions are infinite)
.expectNextMatches(predicate): verify element satisfies condition

exercises.title: Sinks.Many for event-driven subscriptions
exercises.instructions:
Write a Razorpay payment status subscription using Sinks.Many:
1. PaymentEventPublisher: Sinks.Many<PaymentStatus>
2. publishStatus(paymentId, status): push to all subscribers
3. statusUpdates(): Flux from the sink
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): published events received by subscriber
hints: Sinks.many().multicast(): multiple subscribers can consume the same events
tryEmitNext(): non-blocking push (returns result enum, not exception)
.take(1): limit flux for test (prevent blocking forever)

exercises.title: Payment status subscription with takeUntil
exercises.instructions:
Build Razorpay payment status subscription:
1. @SubscriptionMapping paymentStatus(paymentId): filter+takeUntil
2. Filter: only this paymentId's events
3. takeUntil: stop when status is CAPTURED or FAILED
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): receives right payment, stops on terminal status, ignores other payments
hints: .filter(s -> s.paymentId().equals(paymentId)): route events to correct client
.takeUntil(isFinal): inclusive — emits the terminal event, then completes
StepVerifier.then(): trigger side effects during verification

exercises.title: WebSocket configuration for GraphQL
exercises.instructions:
Write Spring for GraphQL WebSocket configuration:
1. application.yml: spring.graphql.websocket.path
2. SecurityConfig: permit WebSocket endpoint
3. Demonstrate: subscription schema definition
4. @SpringBootTest: configuration loads correctly
hints: spring.graphql.websocket.path: configures the WebSocket upgrade endpoint
graphql-ws: the protocol (different from graphql-transport-ws)
spring-boot-starter-websocket: required dependency for WebSocket support

exercises.title: Zerodha live trade subscription with filtering
exercises.instructions:
Build live trade subscription for Zerodha:
1. Trade record: id, symbol, qty, price, type (BUY/SELL), accountId
2. TradeEventBus: Sinks.Many<Trade>
3. @SubscriptionMapping tradeUpdates(accountId): filter by accountId, no takeUntil
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): receives own trades, filters other account, receives multiple
hints: Admin mode (null accountId): subscribe to all trades
.filter(accountId.equals): each subscriber only sees their own trades
StepVerifier.then(): trigger events after subscription is set up

exercises.title: Subscription with error handling
exercises.instructions:
Add error handling to a subscription Flux:
1. priceUpdate(symbol): if symbol is invalid, emit error
2. .onErrorResume(): recover with a fallback Flux
3. .doOnError(): log the error before recovery
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): valid symbol works, invalid gets fallback, error is logged
hints: Flux.defer(): evaluate the condition lazily (when subscribed, not when created)
.doOnError(): side effect on error (logging) — doesn't change error flow
.onErrorResume(): recover with a fallback Flux (catches the error)

exercises.title: Subscription authentication — filter by JWT
exercises.instructions:
Secure subscriptions: only auth'd users see their own data:
1. SubscriptionAuthService: validateToken(token) → accountId
2. @SubscriptionMapping tradeUpdates(@Header token, @Argument accountId)
3. If token invalid: Flux.error()
4. If token valid: filter to matching accountId
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): valid token receives trades, invalid token errors
hints: Flux.error(): immediately emits error (client receives GraphQL error message)
Token validation before Flux creation: fail-fast (don't subscribe to bus)
accountId check: user can't request another account's trades

exercises.title: Subscription lifecycle — connect, receive, disconnect
exercises.expected_solution:
1. doOnSubscribe triggered:
   When a client WebSocket connects and sends a GraphQL subscribe message
   Spring for GraphQL calls the @SubscriptionMapping method
   The returned Flux is subscribed to (Subscriber.onSubscribe is called)
   doOnSubscribe fires at that moment
   Use for: incrementing connection counters, logging 'user X subscribed to Y'

2. doOnCancel triggered:
   When the WebSocket connection is closed (user closes tab/app)
   OR client sends a 'complete' message (intentional unsubscribe)
   OR server cancels the subscription (timeout, auth expiry)
   Spring for GraphQL propagates the cancellation signal to the Flux
   doOnCancel fires at that moment

3. 1000 users → doOnSubscribe calls:
   1000 doOnSubscribe calls (one per user)
   Each user gets their own Flux subscription
   All 1000 Flux instances share the same priceSource.asFlux() upstream
   BUT each adds its own .filter() and operators on top
   Memory: ~1000 Subscriber objects (lightweight)

4. Browser tab closed:
   WebSocket: TCP connection drops (or WebSocket close frame sent)
   Spring for GraphQL: detects connection closure
   Flux: receives cancel signal (as if Subscriber called cancel())
   doOnCancel fires → unregisterClient(symbol)
   No more data is pushed to this connection (it's gone)
   Upstream priceSource: continues publishing (other subscribers still active)

5. Memory leak risk in doOnCancel:
   registerClient(symbol) must be balanced by unregisterClient(symbol)
   If doOnCancel doesn't clean up:
     registerClient accumulates unlimited entries
     Set<String> activeClients grows forever (memory leak)
   Fix: doOnCancel must remove exactly what doOnSubscribe added
   Also: use ConcurrentHashMap or concurrent set (subscriptions are multi-threaded)

test_cases.expected:
1. doOnSubscribe triggered:
   When a client WebSocket connects and sends a GraphQL subscribe message
   Spring for GraphQL calls the @SubscriptionMapping method
   The returned Flux is subscribed to (Subscriber.onSubscribe is called)
   doOnSubscribe fires at that moment
   Use for: incrementing connection counters, logging 'user X subscribed to Y'

2. doOnCancel triggered:
   When the WebSocket connection is closed (user closes tab/app)
   OR client sends a 'complete' message (intentional unsubscribe)
   OR server cancels the subscription (timeout, auth expiry)
   Spring for GraphQL propagates the cancellation signal to the Flux
   doOnCancel fires at that moment

3. 1000 users → doOnSubscribe calls:
   1000 doOnSubscribe calls (one per user)
   Each user gets their own Flux subscription
   All 1000 Flux instances share the same priceSource.asFlux() upstream
   BUT each adds its own .filter() and operators on top
   Memory: ~1000 Subscriber objects (lightweight)

4. Browser tab closed:
   WebSocket: TCP connection drops (or WebSocket close frame sent)
   Spring for GraphQL: detects connection closure
   Flux: receives cancel signal (as if Subscriber called cancel())
   doOnCancel fires → unregisterClient(symbol)
   No more data is pushed to this connection (it's gone)
   Upstream priceSource: continues publishing (other subscribers still active)

5. Memory leak risk in doOnCancel:
   registerClient(symbol) must be balanced by unregisterClient(symbol)
   If doOnCancel doesn't clean up:
     registerClient accumulates unlimited entries
     Set<String> activeClients grows forever (memory leak)
   Fix: doOnCancel must remove exactly what doOnSubscribe added
   Also: use ConcurrentHashMap or concurrent set (subscriptions are multi-threaded)

hints: doOnSubscribe: called once per subscriber when subscription starts
doOnCancel: cleanup hook — prevents memory leaks
1000 subscribers: 1000 independent Flux subscriptions, shared upstream

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha live trading dashboard subscriptions
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Zerodha subscription system:
1. TradeEventBus + PriceEventBus: Sinks.Many sources
2. @SubscriptionMapping tradeUpdates(accountId): filter by account
3. @SubscriptionMapping priceUpdate(symbol): Flux.interval + price simulation
4. Subscription registry: count active subscribers per symbol
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): trade received, price emits, registry tracks, cleanup on cancel
hints: doOnSubscribe+doOnCancel: maintain registry counts (increment/decrement)
blockLast(): subscribe and wait for Flux to complete (OK in tests)
take(5): make infinite Flux finite for tests

exercises.title: Swiggy order tracking subscription
exercises.instructions:
Build Swiggy order tracking with subscription lifecycle:
1. OrderStatus: PLACED → CONFIRMED → PREPARING → OUT_FOR_DELIVERY → DELIVERED
2. @SubscriptionMapping orderStatus(orderId): takeUntil DELIVERED
3. Simulate order progression via scheduled Flux
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): receives all statuses, stops at DELIVERED, correct orderId
hints: takeUntil(isFinal): inclusive — DELIVERED is emitted, then stream completes
EXTRA after DELIVERED: takeUntil stops the Flux so EXTRA is never received
filter by orderId: SWG-OTHER events are ignored (filtered out before client)

exercises.title: Complete payment + trade subscription system
exercises.instructions:
Build complete multi-subscription system:
1. Two independent Sinks.Many: PaymentBus, TradeBus
2. @SubscriptionMapping paymentStatus(paymentId): filter+takeUntil(isFinal)
3. @SubscriptionMapping tradeUpdates(accountId): filter by accountId
4. SecureCheck: both validate a userId before subscribing
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): payment stream, trade stream, invalid user error, concurrent subscriptions
hints: Two independent buses: payments and trades don't interfere
Auth before returning Flux: fail-fast security (Flux.error = immediate error event)
EXTRA after CAPTURED: takeUntil stops → EXTRA never received

exercises.title: Subscription backpressure and slow consumers
exercises.instructions:
Handle slow consumer backpressure:
1. FastPublisher: publishes 1000 events quickly
2. SlowSubscriberSimulator: takes 50ms per event
3. onBackpressureBuffer(100): buffer up to 100 events
4. onBackpressureDrop: drop overflowed events (with counter)
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): fast events, buffer fills, overflow counted
hints: onBackpressureDrop: lose events when subscriber is slow (OK for prices, bad for payments)
onBackpressureBuffer(N): hold N events in memory before dropping
Real backpressure: happens when subscriber thread is slower than publisher

exercises.title: Complete M27 T4 Subscriptions synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M27 T4 synthesis:
1. PaymentStatusSub: filter+takeUntil (Sinks.Many source)
2. PriceSub: Flux.interval, filter by symbol
3. SubscriberRegistry: track active subscription count
4. Auth validation: invalid user → Flux.error
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): payment stream, price stream, auth error, registry tracked
hints: doOnSubscribe+doOnCancel: inc/dec registry counts
blockLast(): subscribes, waits for completion (OK in tests for finite Flux)
takeUntil(isFinal): payment subscription stops automatically on terminal status
